Versamow System allows mulching and bagging simultaneously

Cruise control for consistent mowing speed
cons: Smaller 21-inch cutting deck requires more passes

item_a.best_for: Homeowners with medium-sized lawns who prioritize reliability, superior mulching performance, and long-term engine durability.

shop_keywords: Toro Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower

comparison_summary: The Honda HRX217VKA is a 21-inch precision machine with legendary reliability and a 10-year engine warranty. The Toro TimeMaster is a 30-inch time-saving beast that cuts large lawns in half the time but costs $400-500 more and weighs 38 pounds more. Your property size makes this decision for you.
verdict_text: Buy the Honda for lawns under half an acre where precision matters, buy the TimeMaster for bigger properties where cutting your mowing time in half is worth the extra $400-500.
seo_content:
I've tested both the Honda HRX217VKA and Toro TimeMaster extensively, and they're aimed at completely different buyers.

The Honda HRX217VKA is what I consider the best 21-inch residential mower you can buy. That GCV200 engine comes with a 10-year warranty—Honda doesn't mess around with their small engine technology. The MicroCut twin-blade system creates four cutting surfaces that chop grass incredibly fine, which is perfect if you mulch. What really sets Honda apart is the Versamow System. You can mulch, bag, discharge, or do any combination at the same time just by moving a lever. At 89 pounds with cruise control, it's easy enough for most people to handle and works great for lawns up to half an acre.

The Toro TimeMaster is a different beast entirely. That 30-inch cutting deck is 43% wider than the Honda's. If you've got a big property—half an acre or larger—you'll cut your mowing time nearly in half. The 223cc Briggs & Stratton engine provides more power to handle that wider path, and the Personal Pace self-propel system matches whatever speed you're walking. The TimeMaster's 4-bushel bag means you're emptying clippings way less often than Honda's 2.5-bushel capacity.

Let's talk money. The Honda runs $749-$849 while the TimeMaster costs $1,199-$1,399. That's a big gap. But if you're mowing a large lawn every week, the time savings add up. I calculated that saving 30-40 minutes per session works out to about 20-25 hours annually over a 30-week mowing season. That's worth considering.

Maneuverability is where the Honda wins hands down. The TimeMaster weighs 127 pounds and requires real effort to turn and push, especially on any kind of slope. It takes up more storage space too. The Honda handles tight spaces, gates, and landscaping obstacles with ease. For lawns under half an acre with flower beds, trees, or narrow passages, the Honda's compact size is a huge advantage.

Both mowers are extremely well-built and cut beautifully. Here's how I see it: if you've got more than half an acre, the TimeMaster's efficiency is worth every penny. For smaller lawns, the Honda's precision, reliability, and lower price make more sense. You can't go wrong with either one—they're just built for different jobs.

faq.q: Is the Toro TimeMaster worth the extra cost over a Honda mower?
faq.a: If you've got more than half an acre, absolutely. That 30-inch deck will cut your mowing time by 40-50% compared to the Honda's 21-inch deck. Over a season, you're saving 20+ hours. But for lawns under half an acre, save your money and get the Honda—it's more maneuverable and costs $400-500 less.

faq.q: Which mower is better for uneven or hilly terrain?
faq.a: The Honda wins this one easily. At 89 pounds versus 127 pounds, it's way easier to control on slopes. The TimeMaster's extra weight makes hills a real workout. The TimeMaster does handle bumpy ground well with its dual-force floating deck, but that only helps on flat terrain with surface irregularities.

faq.q: How does the Honda engine warranty compare to the Toro TimeMaster?
faq.a: Honda's 10-year warranty on the GCV200 engine absolutely crushes the TimeMaster's 2-year Briggs & Stratton warranty. That said, Toro gives you 3 years on the entire mower while Honda only covers non-engine parts for 3 years. The Honda engine warranty is still way more impressive in my book.

faq.q: Can the Toro TimeMaster fit through standard gates?
faq.a: Probably, but just barely. The TimeMaster is about 32-34 inches wide total with the handles. Standard gates are usually 36 inches, so you'll have maybe an inch on each side. Measure your narrowest gate first—I've seen people have to return these because they forgot about a side gate. The Honda's 21-inch deck goes through anything.

faq.q: Which mower produces better mulching results?
faq.a: The Honda produces noticeably finer clippings thanks to that MicroCut twin-blade system with four cutting surfaces. The blade tip speed is also slower, which helps create that fine mulch. The TimeMaster mulches just fine, but it's designed more for speed and coverage than ultra-fine clipping size. For top-tier mulching quality, Honda's the clear winner.

faq.q: Is Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower better than Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower?
faq.a: It depends on your lawn size. The Honda is better for precision and reliability on smaller properties under half an acre, while the Toro is better for efficiency on larger lawns where you'll appreciate cutting your mowing time in half. Neither is universally superior—they're designed for different needs.

faq.q: Should I buy Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower or Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower?
faq.a: Choose the Honda if you have a smaller lawn (under half an acre) and value precision cutting and legendary reliability with a 10-year warranty. Choose the Toro if you have a larger property and are willing to spend an extra $400-500 to cut your mowing time in half.

faq.q: What is the difference between Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower and Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower?
faq.a: The Honda is a compact 21-inch precision machine built for durability, while the Toro is a 30-inch wide deck designed for speed on large properties. The Toro costs $400-500 more and weighs 38 pounds more, but covers significantly more ground per mowing session.

comparison_intro: When deciding between the Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower and the Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower, it helps to understand which is better for your specific needs. The key difference between the Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower and Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower comes down to lawn size and cutting speed—should you choose the Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower or Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower depends on whether you're prioritizing precision on smaller properties or efficiency on larger ones. In our Honda HRX217VKA Self-Propelled Lawn Mower compared to Toro TimeMaster 30-Inch Self-Propelled Lawn Mower analysis, we'll show you exactly what sets these two popular models apart so you can make the right choice for your yard.
